children.title: Preprocessing, compiling, linking
children.description: C++ source goes through three phases: the preprocessor expands macros and includes, the compiler produces object files, and the linker combines them into an executable.

children.title: Translation units
children.description: Each .cpp file is a separate translation unit compiled independently — this is why you include headers (declarations) not other .cpp files (definitions).

children.title: C++ additions over C
children.description: C++ adds classes, templates, references, operator overloading, exceptions, and the STL on top of C — understanding C first makes C++ semantics clearer.

children.title: Name mangling
children.description: C++ mangles function names to encode types for overloading — use extern "C" when interfacing with C code to suppress mangling.

children.title: g++ flags
children.description: Key flags: -std=c++20, -O2 (optimize), -Wall -Wextra (warnings), -g (debug info) — always compile with warnings enabled and treat them as errors in production.

children.title: CMake basics
children.description: CMake is the de-facto build system for C++ — cmake_minimum_required, project(), add_executable(), and target_compile_features() cover most project setups.

children.title: VS Code + clangd
children.description: clangd provides C++-aware autocomplete, diagnostics, and go-to-definition — it reads compile_commands.json generated by CMake for accurate project-wide analysis.

children.title: Compiler Explorer
children.description: godbolt.org shows the assembly output of any C++ snippet across multiple compilers and optimization levels — essential for understanding what your code actually compiles to.

children.title: Stack vs heap
children.description: Local variables live on the stack and are automatically destroyed when they go out of scope; heap allocations with new persist until delete — mismanaging them causes leaks.

children.title: Pointer arithmetic
children.description: Incrementing a pointer moves it by sizeof(T) bytes — this is how array indexing works under the hood, and why pointer+offset is equivalent to array subscript.

children.title: Dangling pointers and use-after-free
children.description: Using a pointer after the object it pointed to was destroyed is undefined behaviour — the program may appear to work, crash, or be exploited silently.

children.title: UB and compiler optimizations
children.description: Compilers assume UB never happens and optimize accordingly — this can silently remove safety checks. -fsanitize=address catches many UB cases at runtime.

resources.label: What Every C Programmer Should Know About UB

children.title: Valgrind memcheck
children.description: Valgrind detects heap corruption, use-after-free, and memory leaks at runtime — run with valgrind --leak-check=full on any program handling dynamic memory.

children.title: AddressSanitizer
children.description: ASan is a fast compiler-based sanitizer (compile with -fsanitize=address) that detects buffer overflows and use-after-free with minimal overhead.

children.title: RAII intro
children.description: Wrapping a resource (file, mutex, connection) in a class whose destructor releases it guarantees cleanup even on exceptions — the core C++ resource management pattern.

children.title: Rule of Three
children.description: If you define a destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment operator, you probably need all three — missing one leads to double-free or shallow copy bugs.

children.title: Resource acquisition is initialization
children.description: RAII binds resource lifetime to object lifetime — the constructor acquires the resource and the destructor releases it, making leaks structurally impossible.

children.title: Destructor guarantees
children.description: Destructors are called automatically when an object goes out of scope or is deleted — this makes them the right place to release any resource the constructor acquired.

children.title: vtable mechanism
children.description: Virtual functions are dispatched through a vtable — a per-class array of function pointers. Each virtual call is a two-pointer dereference, which is why virtual dispatch has a (small) cost.

children.title: Virtual destructor
children.description: Always declare destructors virtual in base classes — without it, deleting a derived class through a base pointer calls only the base destructor, leaking the derived part.

children.title: Pure virtual interfaces
children.description: A class with only pure virtual methods (= 0) is an abstract interface — the standard C++ way to define a contract without any implementation.

children.title: Composition over inheritance
children.description: Deep inheritance hierarchies are fragile in C++ — prefer composing objects and using interfaces over long inheritance chains.

children.title: Copy and move semantics
children.description: The Rule of Five extends Rule of Three with the move constructor and move assignment operator — implementing move avoids unnecessary copies for expensive resources.

children.title: noexcept specifier
children.description: Marking move constructors noexcept allows STL containers to use them for reallocation instead of falling back to copying — a key performance optimization.

children.title: Big-O for STL ops
children.description: std::vector provides O(1) random access and amortized O(1) push_back; std::map provides O(log n) ops; std::unordered_map provides O(1) average — choosing the right container matters.

children.title: Iterator categories
children.description: Iterators come in 6 categories (input, output, forward, bidirectional, random access, contiguous) — algorithms require specific categories, and using the wrong one causes compile errors.

children.title: Predicate-based algorithms
children.description: STL algorithms like std::sort and std::find_if accept predicates — passing lambdas as predicates makes algorithms reusable for any comparison logic.

children.title: Range concepts (C++20)
children.description: C++20 ranges provide lazy, composable views over containers — std::ranges::filter(v, pred) | std::ranges::transform(f) replaces manual iterator loops.

children.title: vector vs deque vs list
children.description: vector is contiguous and cache-friendly for sequential access; deque supports efficient front/back insertion; list has O(1) splicing but poor cache performance.

children.title: unordered_map vs map
children.description: unordered_map provides O(1) average lookup using hashing; map provides O(log n) with sorted keys — use unordered_map unless you need ordering or a custom comparator.

children.title: std::sort and std::stable_sort
children.description: std::sort provides introsort (O(n log n) worst case); std::stable_sort preserves relative order of equal elements — prefer sort unless stability is required.

children.title: std::transform and std::for_each
children.description: transform applies a function to each element and writes results to another range; for_each applies a function for side effects — both replace explicit loops.

children.title: Compile-time code generation
children.description: Templates generate code at compile time for each type they're instantiated with — this is zero-runtime-overhead abstraction, but can increase binary size.

children.title: Template bloat
children.description: Instantiating the same template with many types creates many copies of the generated code — explicit instantiation declarations and the pimpl idiom mitigate this.

children.title: Substitution failure is not an error
children.description: When template argument deduction fails for one overload, the compiler silently tries the next — this is the mechanism behind type-based function selection.

children.title: std::enable_if
children.description: enable_if<condition, T>::type disables a template overload when condition is false — the classic SFINAE tool, largely replaced by concepts in C++20.

children.title: CRTP — Curiously Recurring Template Pattern
children.description: CRTP passes the derived class as a template argument to the base — enabling static polymorphism (no vtable) and mixin-style code reuse.

children.title: Policy-based design
children.description: Policy classes are template parameters that inject behaviour — separating concerns like allocation strategy from container logic for zero-cost customisation.

children.title: requires clauses
children.description: requires(T t) { t.size(); } specifies what operations a type must support — much clearer template errors than SFINAE, with better IDE support.

children.title: Standard concepts
children.description: The <concepts> header provides ready-made concepts (std::integral, std::floating_point, std::invocable) — use them instead of writing your own SFINAE checks.

children.title: unique_ptr ownership model
children.description: unique_ptr represents exclusive ownership — it cannot be copied, only moved. When it goes out of scope, it automatically deletes the managed object.

children.title: shared_ptr reference counting
children.description: shared_ptr uses atomic reference counting — when the last copy is destroyed, the managed object is deleted. The shared control block has overhead; use unique_ptr when ownership is exclusive.

children.title: Breaking reference cycles
children.description: shared_ptr cycles prevent deletion — weak_ptr observes a shared_ptr without owning it, breaking the cycle and allowing proper cleanup.

children.title: lock() pattern
children.description: weak_ptr::lock() returns a shared_ptr if the managed object still exists, or an empty shared_ptr if it was already destroyed — always check before using.

children.title: make_unique and make_shared
children.description: Always use make_unique<T>(args) instead of new — it's exception-safe (no leak if constructor throws) and more readable. make_shared allocates the object and control block together.

children.title: Passing smart pointers
children.description: Pass by raw reference/pointer when ownership isn't transferred; pass unique_ptr by value to transfer ownership; pass shared_ptr by value to share ownership — passing by const& just to observe.

children.title: File handle RAII with unique_ptr
children.description: unique_ptr<FILE, decltype(&fclose)> wraps a C FILE* and calls fclose automatically — any custom cleanup can be injected as a deleter.

children.title: Array deleters
children.description: unique_ptr<T[]> automatically uses delete[] instead of delete for array allocations — use it when you must manage raw arrays (rare with std::vector).

children.title: Closure object
children.description: A lambda creates an anonymous struct (closure type) with operator() — capturing variables copies or references them as data members of this struct.

children.title: Capture modes
children.description: [=] captures all locals by value; [&] by reference; [this] captures the this pointer — mixing them ([=, &x]) is allowed. Reference captures dangle if the lambda outlives the local.

children.title: Lazy evaluation
children.description: Range views are lazy — they don't compute anything until you iterate. Chaining views composes into a single pass over the data without intermediate containers.

children.title: Views vs actions
children.description: Views are lazy and don't modify data; actions (range-v3 concept) eagerly consume and produce new containers. The distinction matters for performance and lifetime.

children.title: Structured bindings (C++17)
children.description: auto [x, y] = pair; binds multiple variables from a struct, pair, or tuple in one line — cleaner than std::tie and works with custom types implementing get<>.

children.title: if constexpr
children.description: if constexpr(condition) evaluates the condition at compile time and discards the dead branch entirely — enabling template specialization logic without enable_if.

children.title: std::views::filter and transform
children.description: std::views::filter(pred) | std::views::transform(f) composes into a lazy pipeline — iterate once, no intermediate allocations, and composable with any range.

children.title: std::ranges algorithms
children.description: std::ranges::sort, std::ranges::find_if, and std::ranges::copy replace their iterator-pair counterparts — accepting containers directly and projections for member access.

children.title: Happens-before and memory orderings
children.description: C++'s memory model defines happens-before — std::memory_order controls how atomic operations synchronize between threads without locks.

children.title: Data races are UB
children.description: A data race (unsynchronized access where at least one is a write) is undefined behaviour in C++ — the compiler and CPU may reorder operations in ways that make the race worse.

children.title: Thread sanitizer (TSan)
children.description: Compile with -fsanitize=thread to detect data races at runtime — TSan instruments memory accesses and reports races with the exact thread and location.

children.title: std::atomic for lock-free programming
children.description: std::atomic<T> provides lock-free operations on simple types — use it instead of mutex for hot counters and flags where lock overhead matters.

children.title: std::thread
children.description: std::thread launches a function on a new OS thread — always join() or detach() before the thread object is destroyed to avoid std::terminate being called.

children.title: std::async and std::future
children.description: std::async launches a task asynchronously (or deferred) and returns a future — a higher-level alternative to raw threads for tasks that produce a result.

children.title: std::mutex and lock_guard
children.description: std::lock_guard<std::mutex> is an RAII wrapper that locks on construction and unlocks on destruction — the standard pattern for protecting shared state.

children.title: std::condition_variable
children.description: condition_variable allows one thread to wait until a condition is true — use with a unique_lock and a predicate to avoid spurious wakeups.

children.title: Basic, strong, nothrow guarantees
children.description: Exception safety levels: basic (invariants hold), strong (commit-or-rollback), nothrow (never throws). Most well-written C++ targets the strong guarantee for mutating operations.

children.title: RAII for exception safety
children.description: RAII objects clean up automatically when an exception unwinds the stack — this is what makes C++ exception handling practical without finally blocks.

children.title: Result type pattern
children.description: std::expected<T, E> either holds a value or an error — a zero-overhead alternative to exceptions for recoverable errors in performance-critical paths.

children.title: Error handling without exceptions
children.description: Embedded and real-time C++ often disables exceptions — std::expected, error_code, and output parameters are the alternatives with deterministic performance.

children.title: try/catch hierarchies
children.description: Catch exceptions from most specific to least specific — catching std::exception& first handles most standard library errors with a readable .what() message.

children.title: Custom exception classes
children.description: Derive from std::exception or std::runtime_error to create domain-specific exceptions — override what() to return a meaningful error description.

children.title: noexcept in move operations
children.description: Move constructors and move assignment operators should be noexcept — STL containers check this to decide whether to move or copy during reallocation.

children.title: Performance implications
children.description: noexcept functions allow the compiler to skip generating exception-handling table entries — measurable improvement in binary size and sometimes in performance.

children.title: Modern CMake patterns
children.description: target_* commands (target_include_directories, target_link_libraries) with PRIVATE/PUBLIC/INTERFACE propagate dependencies correctly — never use global include_directories().

children.title: Conan package manager
children.description: Conan manages C++ dependencies with versioning and binary caching — integrates with CMake via conan_cmake_run() or the modern CMakeDeps generator.

children.title: gprof and perf
children.description: gprof instruments binaries at compile time; perf samples CPU counters with no recompilation — perf is more accurate for production code.

children.title: Compiler Sanitizers
children.description: -fsanitize=address,undefined,thread catches memory errors, UB, and data races respectively — run all three in your CI pipeline before shipping.

children.title: UBSan and ASan in CI
children.description: Running tests with -fsanitize=address,undefined in CI catches memory and UB issues that only show up in specific inputs — the highest ROI compiler flag for correctness.

children.title: ThreadSanitizer
children.description: -fsanitize=thread detects data races at runtime with about 5-15x slowdown — run on the concurrent parts of your test suite to catch races before they reach production.

children.title: GitHub Actions for C++
children.description: A CMake + CTest workflow builds and tests in CI — add separate jobs for each sanitizer and for multiple compilers (GCC, Clang) to catch compiler-specific issues.

children.title: clang-tidy static analysis
children.description: clang-tidy provides hundreds of C++ lint checks — run it in CI with -warnings-as-errors to enforce coding style and catch common bugs before review.
